item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just stumbled upon this SCP, and it's one of the better ones I've read. It's a creative anomaly, a great inversion of the usual Zachary C-format, and it shows how the Foundation can be duped. The formatting isn't much of a deal breaker to me, and I actually really like this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this a lot, in a lot of ways.

And contrary to some people, I doubt they are getting too much from the Foundation, maybe twice the budget they would expect, tops.

This still relies on a solid ground: some information, even if very general, can still get out of the city, and the Foundation can send reliable agents to see what's happening. But the local team is actually doing their jobs: this is certainly an anomaly, and doing research is their job. Object Classes are very hard to define in some cases, and something that can't be known outside its imediate vicinity isn't outside the definition of Keter, as harmless as it is, since it can't be reliably contained by the Foundation at large.

Even the statement that dealing with it is hazardous is true, since they can have their memories messed up in unpredictable ways.

So, even if someone figures out what is happening, in the end both entries are true, and they are doing the work they are intended to do. They can't be blamed on this work being less dangerous than the anomaly makes it appear to the Foundation, and asking for budget is just what every research team do. At most an auditor could transmit from there their personal opinion on Object Class and on the resources sent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And since the precise budget isn't revealed, or even implied, it isn't necessarily outrageous. Just an undefined "more than it would be given".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Holy crap," I thought just before opening the collapsible. "Somebody figured out a perfect way to goldbrick the Foundation!"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forget Oak Grove. Have you heard about that thing in Samothrace?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine Triple Threat as sounding somewhere between Dilated Peoples and Nappy Roots. Do indie hipsters like them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I need more SCPs that start as Keter and end as Safe. Also, Triple Threat would be a indie hipster's wetdream.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why, what's going on in Oak Grove?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant format screw. I kinda figured, once funding was mentioned, that the Foundation was being duped somehow, but even at the reveal, I can't completely blame these guys, since it seems they actually do want to help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and the reason you don't really know much about what's going on in this article is that you're not in Oak Grove, Kentucky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Methinks you're asking a bit much. It would also up the immersion if you had to hack into the site to read anything- that doesn't make it a good idea. Its important to remember that this isn't some lame creepypasta hoax site looking to make you believe its real. (I will never understand why anyone thinks those are a good idea.) This is a fiction site looking to tell stories. You cannot tell stories if people cannot read them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Triple Threat having five members is sort of a shout-out to the Hitchhiker's Guide "Trilogy". And at one point, it was just the three brothers, so it made sense.

Fifthist tag… eh, up to the tagger, I don't care much either way. And I don't have a 'word of God' to give anyone on what the connection is, because I don't know either. I don't really know much about what's going on in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They have basically no accountability. They can be as non-clinical as they want to; for most of the article, it suits them for conveying information, but if they have something they want to say that falls outside of that, there's nobody telling them they can't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was going to neutral vote because of your insistence on breaking format, but having read your reasoning above, I can buy "Anomaly Class" being used here. Upvoted.

Minor nitpick: calling Triple Threat's music high-quality seem rather nonclinical, as does the note at the end (though not to the point of breaking immersion).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I just noticed that Tripple Threat has five members…

Someone should add the Fifthist tag. Part of the reason I enjoy the researchers blowing it off is because they are 100% blissfully unaware of the Fifthist connection, and are probably under its effects.

Edit: Just to be clear, I'm not saying that the Fifthist tag is necessary to the article, just that it would be more fun. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I want people to actually be able to read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "You Probably Haven't Heard of Them"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well you probably could have made some kind of mirror that is only accessible if your IP orginates in Oak Grove… but that would be a lot of work and so incredibly meta almost nobody would get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The trope inversion was absolutely deliberate. It was a cool plot device with Zachary Callahan, but by now it's been greatly diluted to the point where it can't possibly be dramatic at all, and indeed is just not being used to good effect any more. I'm hoping people will take this as a cue to try fresher things with that format.

As for the Anomaly Class… sure, why not, alternate Foundationverse. I do think that the Oak Grove research team felt justified in taking a few liberties with the format, since they're the only ones reading it, which is why it exists alongside the 'Object Class' used by the Foundation at large.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've edited the line in the containment procedures to your suggestion.

'Considering' and 'will' are not used together in that paragraph. As far as I can tell, the conjugation is appropriate.

I've fixed the third issue.

Thank you for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't be the only one who clicked on the "original document link" and basically just refreshed the page am I?

Awesome job Scantron

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand this objection, but I unfortunately don't see much of a way around it. I would much rather tell this story by breaking immersion a bit than not tell it at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, it breaks immersion. I thought one of the points of SCPs was to be convincing, so people who don't know any better might believe the documents are real. I remember an old deleted SCP which basically was "a thing right behind you" and was saved because it was considered one of the worst SCPs of all time, one of the reasons was because it was so easy to disprove (just look behind you and there's no monster. Although TBH reading it kind of did creep me out.) I don't see anyone reading this and believing they actually visited the town.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm open to recommendations on a clever mainlist name.

"Oak Grove Kentucky and the Triple Threat?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
results in a malfunction either in the device sending the data, preventing the transmission.

This "either" is lonely without an "or" to sleep with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe that's the case, but if so, it doesn't really work. It's not really a format issue, it just doesn't make sense. That'd fit in a tale maybe, but here it doesn't really gel. Not in what is ostensibly a containment report on a Foundation database.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I thought 'what a short scp…' but when I read on, I clicked the link and the page expanded!
I love it when scps do that! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how it inverts the usual "Safe, but it is actually Keter and we need to hide it or else" trope played in many SCPs.

And Scantron, I got this question: What's with the "Anomaly Class" thing? Are all your SCPs set in an alternate Foundation or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. He'll, I was set to upvote when I saw Constellation Starfish and made the connection. And bonus points for reminding me of Nexuses like Sloth's Pit and Gravity Falls.

Also, in my headcanon, some of the info declaring it extremely dangerous comes from leaving researchers conflating it with past SCPs that were Keter/nasty in their debriefings

That said, a few minor things (and please bear with me, because I'm on my phone):

Last line of the containment procedures: It's currently in the form "Contact the budgeting office for a list…" I think it would sound more professional if it were "For a list…, contact the budgeting office."

In the description where you were talking about the cognitohazardous effect of finding their abilities normal, there was a conjugation issue with "considering" and "will".

In the paragraph describing Triple Threat, you missed a -A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The bottom collapsible is meant to represent you actually going to the town and reading about it.

Kinda… fourth-wall breaking, but hard to do it any other way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how they explode?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I have to ask…

So how am I reading about it then?

The 'requires an in-person visit' doesn't hold up against the anomalous effect of preventing information regarding the skip in any format from leaving the town. Unless I personally went to the town to read this, I shouldn't be able to have this information, right?

I think you built too perfect of a prison here. I'm going to re-read to see if I missed something, but simply because it's (mostly) technically sound and well written I will be no voting for now.

Edit: Upon rereading I see that I forgot the note that some very general information is able to be communicated, but I think that explanation is frankly a little too convenient. The information in this article isn't general at all, there's a lot of very specific info (pretty much any info you'd need to write a skip) so that by the end it didn't even seem like there were any antimemetic properties whatsoever.

I think a LOT of this info would need to be removed for them to be convincingly anomalous, because right now I know everything I would need to know about these kids, as far as I can tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you know that something similar isn't happening to you, except in webspace instead of meatspace?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Memories of SCP-2204-B are consistently replaced with memories of material from the Austin Powers franchise or from the nonexistent rock band Constellation Starfish.

or from the nonexistent rock band Constellation Starfish.

Motherfucker! I didn't even notice this!

I love this article, and the idea of you and your music not being able to be known outside of where you live is immensely terrifying to me, as well as hitting close in some way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally, I write something that isn't a coldpost.

Thanks to a few people (Decibelles, TwistedGears, Cimmerian, I forget who else) in chat who reviewed this.

I'm open to recommendations on a clever mainlist name. I'm choosing Triple Threat: Intracommunal Men of Obscurity. Because more Austin Powers references.

To head off a few questions:

  • The idea is that the collapsible is meant to represent you heading out to Oak Grove and reading their item summary. The 'real' document is not actually in the main SCP database; I would have used the main article as just a splash page, but I figured it would be much simpler to just have it all on one page.
  • As for the Austin Powers motif: Why not?
